content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompting to Mitigate Social Biases in Language Models",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate outputs that reinforce social biases and stereotypes, even when explicitly instructed to be neutral. Existing prompting methods often fail to control these biases reliably.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, ensuring equitable and unbiased outputs is critical for ethical AI adoption. Recent work shows that simple prompt engineering is insufficient, and more robust, context-aware techniques are needed to address persistent biases, especially given the rapid scaling and deployment of LLMs.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting mechanism: Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompts (CSRP). Each user prompt is automatically paired with a counterfactual version that swaps social identities (e.g., gender, ethnicity) in relevant entities. The LLM is prompted to generate responses for both the original and counterfactual prompts, then reflectively compare its outputs, explicitly identifying and correcting any bias or stereotype in its own generation. This is achieved by appending a meta-instruction that requires the model to examine differences in generated content and revise responses to minimize bias.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CSRP on the BOLD and StereoSet datasets, comparing it against state-of-the-art debiasing prompts, including Chain-of-Thought and Direct Bias Mitigation prompts. Bias and stereotype scores will be measured using established metrics such as SEAT and StereoSet’s language model bias score, as well as human judgment. The primary metric is reduction in measured bias without harming task relevance or fluency."
}
